name: SCWorx Corp.

profile.description: SCWorx Corp. provides cloud-based SaaS data management, normalization, and interoperability solutions for healthcare providers in the United States, primarily supporting hospital Workday ERP implementations with item master file repair, charge description master management, and AI-assisted data governance services. The company has 7 employees, $2.88M in trailing twelve-month revenue, a net loss of $4.44M, and a market cap under $400K. Nasdaq suspended trading on April 14, 2026 for failure to maintain the $1.00 minimum bid price; the company implemented a 1-for-15 reverse stock split on April 10, 2026 and has appealed the delisting determination.

innovation.label: WEAK
innovation.analysis: Track Record (0/3): SCWorx has not launched any meaningful new products, market pivots, or business model transformations in the last five years; the company continues to offer the same healthcare data normalization SaaS platform it has operated since inception, with no expansion beyond its narrow Workday ERP support niche [1][2]. AI Deployment (1/2): The company's SaaS platform incorporates artificial intelligence to 'rapidly repair and expand data' for healthcare providers, but no measurable AI-driven revenue gains, cost savings, or customer metrics have been disclosed in SEC filings or press releases [2][3]. Market Share (0/2): At $2.88M in TTM revenue and 7 employees, SCWorx holds negligible market share in the $300B+ healthcare IT market, competing against Veeva, Informatica, and in-house data teams at major consulting firms [1]. Pipeline (1/2): SCWorx signed a new Southeastern healthcare IDN SaaS agreement and a Midwest academic hospital contract, and Q1 2026 revenue rose modestly to $745K from $720K year-over-year, but no specific next-generation products or launch timelines have been announced [1]. R&D Intensity (0/1): With only 7 employees and no disclosed R&D spending line item, the company cannot sustain meaningful research and development investment [1].

inspiration.label: WEAK
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): SCWorx's purpose — improving healthcare data quality and interoperability to support better clinical and financial outcomes — is clear and meaningful, as poor data quality in hospital systems contributes to billing errors, supply chain waste, and patient safety risks [2]. However, the mission is not distinctive; it describes what the company does rather than articulating a deeper purpose that transcends the business itself. Community Impact (0/2): At 7 employees and a sub-$400K market cap, SCWorx has no corporate philanthropy programs, community investment initiatives, or charitable giving of any kind [1]. Employee Retention (0/2): No employee retention data, tenure statistics, or turnover information is publicly available; the Nasdaq delisting crisis, 1-for-15 reverse split, and stock price collapse from a 52-week high of $21.90 (pre-split adjusted) to $0.36 create extreme uncertainty for any remaining employees [1]. Insider Buying (0/2): No net insider buying has been reported in the last 12 months; given the company's financial distress and going-concern qualification, insider investment would be unusual [1].

infrastructure.label: WEAK
infrastructure.analysis: LIQUIDITY FLOOR FAILURE: avg_daily_volume of 347,060 × stock price of $0.36 = $124,942, which is 87.5% below the $1,000,000 liquidity threshold — Infrastructure score is automatically 0 [4]. For completeness, the underlying metrics are dire: ROE of approximately -98.7% (net loss of $4.44M on $4.5M shareholders' equity) scores 0; profit margin of approximately -154% (net loss of $4.44M on $2.88M revenue) scores 0; Forward P/E and PEG ratio are unavailable due to negative earnings and no analyst coverage [1][4]. The sole bright spot is D/E of 2.6% ($116.7K total debt against $4.5M equity) which would score 1.0, but this is irrelevant given the liquidity failure. Cash of $1.34M at March 31, 2026 against ~$1.5M annual operating burn implies approximately 11 months of runway [1].

ai_resilience.label: HIGH RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (4/9): EXPOSED — SCWorx's core service of healthcare data normalization, item master file repair, and charge description master management is directly threatened by advances in large language models and AI-native data management tools that can perform structured data cleaning, normalization, and interoperability mapping at scale with minimal human oversight [2][3]. Healthcare regulatory complexity (HIPAA, HL7/FHIR standards) and the domain-specific knowledge required for hospital ERP implementations provide partial protection, as AI tools still require human-in-the-loop validation for clinical data, but this moat is eroding as AI capabilities improve quarterly. Hospitals adopting AI-native ERP platforms from Workday, Oracle Health, or Epic may increasingly handle data normalization internally. AI Opportunity (0/1): While SCWorx claims its SaaS platform uses AI for data repair, no quantifiable AI-specific revenue, separate AI product line, or measurable AI-driven customer outcomes have been disclosed in any SEC filing or press release [1].

infinite_game.summary: SCWorx is a micro-cap company in survival mode — fighting Nasdaq delisting, burning through its last $1.34M in cash, and operating with 7 employees. Simon Sinek's Infinite Game framework requires, at minimum, the organizational stability and financial runway to make long-term bets and build enduring capability. SCWorx is playing the most finite game possible: the game of staying alive through the next quarter. Every strategic decision is dictated by short-term survival necessity, not a forward-looking vision of a better future.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (0/3): SCWorx does not articulate a Just Cause that extends beyond its own survival. Improving healthcare data quality is a legitimate goal, but the company's public communications — SEC filings, press releases, investor relations — focus entirely on contract wins, compliance deadlines, and reverse stock splits, not on a vision of a transformed healthcare system [1][2]. There is no evidence that a purpose beyond revenue generation drives strategic decisions. Sinek would classify this as a company that exists to exist.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (0/2): No evidence of courageous long-term investment decisions. CEO Timothy Hannibal's leadership has focused on operational survival — signing incremental SaaS contracts, managing Nasdaq compliance, and executing a reverse stock split to maintain listing status [1][3]. These are necessary tactical decisions, but they represent the opposite of Sinek's courage framework: there are no examples of sacrificing near-term results for long-term capability, because the company cannot afford to sacrifice anything.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (0/2): With only 7 employees operating under Nasdaq delisting pressure, a stock price that has collapsed over 97% from its all-time high, and a going-concern qualification from auditors, it is impossible to assess organizational trust in any meaningful way [1]. The extreme financial stress and existential uncertainty facing the company would make psychological safety and employee empowerment extraordinarily difficult to maintain, regardless of leadership intent.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (0/2): SCWorx has not demonstrated any meaningful strategic pivot or business model transformation since its founding. The company remains in the same narrow niche of healthcare data normalization it has always occupied [2]. No evidence of entering adjacent markets, developing new technology platforms, or making strategic bets that could redefine the company. The 1-for-15 reverse stock split is a financial survival mechanism, not a strategic transformation.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): No evidence of an infinite competitive mindset. SCWorx's public communications do not reference competitors, learning from rivals, or using competitive pressure to improve [1][2]. The company operates in a niche where larger competitors (Veeva, Informatica, Workday's own data tools) could easily subsume its functionality, yet there is no acknowledgment of these dynamics or articulation of how SCWorx differentiates through continuous improvement.

investment_thesis: SCWorx's only constructive thesis is as an acquisition target: the company possesses a functional SaaS platform for healthcare data normalization, documented hospital customer relationships with multi-year contracts (including a 113% value increase on one three-year renewal), and a niche specialization in Workday ERP implementations that a larger healthcare IT firm could bolt on [1]. Q1 2026 showed marginal improvement with revenue of $745K (up from $720K YoY), gross profit nearly tripling to $374K from $137K, and net loss narrowing to $174K from $476K, suggesting the underlying business operations may be stabilizing [1]. The extremely low D/E of 2.6% and zero long-term debt mean an acquirer would inherit a clean balance sheet.
thesis_continued: The bear case is overwhelming and multidimensional. WORX fails IOWN's liquidity threshold by 87.5% ($125K average daily trading value versus the $1M minimum), rendering the stock essentially untradeable for any institutional investor [4]. Nasdaq suspended trading on April 14, 2026 after the stock failed to maintain the $1.00 minimum bid price despite a 1-for-15 reverse split; the company has appealed but delisting to OTC markets would further destroy liquidity and institutional access [1]. Cash of $1.34M at March 31, 2026 against ~$1.5M in annual operating burn gives approximately 11 months of runway before requiring dilutive capital raises or shutting down [1]. The $4.44M net loss on $2.88M revenue (FY2025) represents a company that loses $1.54 for every $1 it earns. AI-native data management tools are increasingly capable of performing the exact healthcare data normalization that constitutes SCWorx's entire business [3].
key_catalysts: Successful Nasdaq delisting appeal and compliance with listing requirements through sustained price recovery above $1.00 post-reverse-split, which is a prerequisite for continued public market access and any institutional investment

Acceleration of new SaaS customer acquisitions in the Workday healthcare ERP implementation market, building on Q1 2026 gross profit improvement to $374K (up 173% YoY) toward a sustainable breakeven trajectory

Strategic acquisition by a larger healthcare IT or SaaS consolidator (Veeva, Informatica, Workday partner ecosystem) that values the platform, customer relationships, and domain expertise at a premium to the sub-$400K market cap
key_risks: Nasdaq delisting finalization: trading was suspended April 14, 2026 and the company's appeal is the last recourse; failure would relegate shares to OTC markets where liquidity and institutional access are virtually nonexistent, likely triggering further price collapse

Cash runway exhaustion: $1.34M cash at March 31, 2026 against ~$1.5M annual operating burn implies approximately 11 months of runway, after which the company must raise dilutive capital (destroying existing shareholders) or cease operations entirely

AI commoditization of healthcare data normalization: large language models and AI-native data platforms from Workday, Oracle Health, and Epic are increasingly capable of performing the structured data repair, normalization, and interoperability mapping that constitutes SCWorx's entire revenue base
